# Host-Pathogen Interaction Analysis

## Uncovering how pathogens cause disease

### NGS facilitates studies of host and pathogen gene expression and the influence of the microbiome

![](//assets.illumina.com/content/dam/illumina-marketing/images/areas-of-interest/infectious-disease/host-pathogen-interactions-web-graphic.jpg)

#### Genetics of Host-Pathogen Interactions

Critical to both fighting and finding therapies for infectious diseases is understanding disease pathogenesis, pathogen virulence, and further, how the human microbiome influences responses to pathogens and therapies. Next-generation sequencing (NGS) can help researchers study host-pathogen interactions, uncover how pathogens cause disease, and explore pathogen infection mechanisms. Understanding these factors can inform public health decisions and help identify potential future therapeutic targets.

NGS can also be used in clinical research to address whether the host microbiome influences immunity, vaccine efficacy, or therapy response, as well as study the effects of both pathogen and host gene expression and how this influences host response to a disease or therapy. Key benefits of using Illumina NGS to study the genetics of host-pathogen interactions include:

#### Metagenomics for Host-Pathogen Interaction Studies

Metagenomics involves using NGS to provide taxonomic and functional profiles of microbial communities without
the need to culture the microbes in the lab. This method allows researchers to perform a broad range of
host-pathogen interaction and microbiome studies such as:

##### Shotgun Metagenomics Methods Guide

Gain insight into microbial community biodiversity and function, study virulence genes,
and identify emerging diseases.

##### Shotgun Metagenomics Methods Guide

![](//assets.illumina.com/content/dam/illumina-marketing/images/systems/nextseq-1000-2000/nextseq-1000-2000-gradient-low-angle-crop-web-graphic.jpg)

#### Host-Pathogen Transcriptome Analysis

NGS-based RNA sequencing can uncover valuable information about the activation/deactivation and expression levels of host and pathogen genes and transcripts. These findings can shed light on the role of host-pathogen interactions in pathogenic processes and the biological pathways involved in disease transmission. Furthermore, metatranscriptomic analysis can reveal complex interactions between the host, pathogen, and microbial community members.

#### Scientists Discuss Host-Microbe Interaction Studies

##### The Time is Now for Microbiome Studies

Researchers dissect host-microbial interactions to relate the functions in microbial communities back to
disease and obtain data to refine drug discovery.

##### NGS is Revealing the Mysterious World of Microbes

Dr. Philip Hugenholtz believes understanding the host and microbiome genotype interface will be a
powerful aspect of personalized medicine.

##### Microbiome Multi-Omics and Human Health

The latest sequencing technologies allow researchers to explore what our microbial inhabitants are doing
and how they contribute to, or protect from, diseases.

![](/content/dam/illumina-marketing/ui/icons/icon-lg-document.svg)

###### High-Throughput Microbial Community Profiling

Explore the connection between microbial communities, pathogens, and human health. This streamlined, automated research workflow generates highly uniform libraries and provides excellent data for metagenomic profiling, species identification, and de novo genome assembly.

#### Related Solutions

##### Microbiome Analysis

The study of microbial communities found in and on the human body allows researchers to
understand the role of microbes in health and disease.

##### Shotgun Metagenomics

Analyze all genomic content in a given complex sample. Predict the functional potential of
a microbial community, detect anti-microbial resistance and virulence genes, evaluate microbial diversity, and
analyze microorganisms culture-free.

##### Microbial Transcriptomics

RNA sequencing offers an unbiased approach for analyzing and quantifying bacterial, viral,
and other microbial transcripts.

##### Infectious Diseases & NGS

NGS can identify novel pathogens, distinguish between infectious disease strains that
differ by as little as one SNP, and replace the need for multiple assays.

##### COVID-19 Host Risk & Response

Explore methods for studying host genetic risk factors and immune system responses to the
SARS-CoV-2 virus.

![illumina logo](https://assets.illumina.com/content/dam/illumina-common/logo/illumina-full_logo.svg)

##### For Research Use Only

Not for use in diagnostic procedures (except as specifically noted).

Not for import or sale to the Australian general public.

##### Innovative technologies

At Illumina, our goal is to apply innovative technologies to the analysis of genetic variation and function, making studies possible that were not even imaginable just a few years ago. It is mission critical for us to deliver innovative, flexible, and scalable solutions to meet the needs of our customers. As a global company that places high value on collaborative interactions, rapid delivery of solutions, and providing the highest level of quality, we strive to meet this challenge. Illumina innovative sequencing and array technologies are fueling groundbreaking advancements in life science research, translational and consumer genomics, and molecular diagnostics.

# High-Throughput Transcriptome Analysis for Investigating Host-Pathogen Interactions

### Affiliations

# High-Throughput Transcriptome Analysis for Investigating Host-Pathogen Interactions

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Pathogens can cause a wide variety of infectious diseases. The biological processes induced by the host in response to infection determine the severity of the disease. To study such processes, researchers can use high-throughput sequencing techniques (RNA-seq) that measure the dynamic changes of the host transcriptome at different stages of infection, clinical outcomes, or disease severity.This investigation can lead to a better understanding of the diseases, as well as uncovering potential drug targets and treatments. The protocol presented here describes a complete pipeline to analyze RNA-sequencing data from raw reads to functional analysis. The pipeline is divided into five steps: (1) quality control of the data; (2) mapping and annotation of genes; (3) statistical analysis to identify differentially expressed genes and co-expressed genes; (4) determination of the molecular degree of the perturbation of samples; and (5) functional analysis. Step 1 removes technical artifacts that may impact the quality of downstream analyses. In step 2, genes are mapped and annotated according to standard library protocols. The statistical analysis in step 3 identifies genes that are differentially expressed or co-expressed in infected samples, in comparison with non-infected ones. Sample variability and the presence of potential biological outliers are verified using the molecular degree of perturbation approach in step 4. Finally, the functional analysis in step 5 reveals the pathways associated with the disease phenotype. The presented pipeline aims to support researchers through the RNA-seq data analysis from host-pathogen interaction studies and drive future in vitro or in vivo experiments, that are essential to understand the molecular mechanism of infections.

Review

Predicting host-pathogen interactions with machine learning algorithms: A scoping review
================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Rasool Sahragard a, Masoud Arabfard b, Ali Najafi a

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.meegid.2025.105751](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.meegid.2025.105751 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S1567134825000401&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •This review evaluates studies on machine learning for HPI prediction, categorizing by host, pathogen, algorithms, and metrics. 
*   •Findings reveal that tree-based algorithms like Random Forest and Gradient Boosting in HPI prediction, while deep learning needs more labeled data. 
*   •The review identifies gaps in dataset standardization and model interpretability, stressing the need for better quality and transparency in AI. 

Abstract
--------

### Background

Diseases caused by pathogenic microorganisms pose a persistent global health challenge. Pathogens exploit host mechanisms through intricate molecular interactions. Understanding these host-pathogen interactions (HPIs), particularly protein-protein interactions (PPIs), is crucial for developing therapeutic strategies. While experimental approaches are essential, they are often labor-intensive and costly. Researchers have been able to predict HPIs more efficiently due to recent advances in artificial intelligence and machine learning. However, existing reviews lack a systematic evaluation of different machine learning methodologies and their effectiveness.

### Methods

This scoping review critically examines recent studies on machine learning-based Host-Pathogen Interaction (HPI) prediction, categorizing them by host and pathogen types, machine learning algorithms, and key evaluation metrics. The methodology is based on the study beginning with a preliminary search in reputable using key phrases related to host-pathogen interactions from 2019 to 2024. This process yielded 46 relevant articles, from which 30 were selected for review after evaluating titles and abstracts.

### Results

Our findings indicate that tree-based algorithms, particularly Random Forest and Gradient Boosting, are the most prevalent in Host-Pathogen Interaction (HPI) prediction. The filter articles were categorized by host and pathogen type and further subdivided into four subcategories based on the prediction type and machine learning algorithms: classic, tree-based, vector-based, and neural network algorithms. Convolutional and recurrent neural networks are among the deep learning models that demonstrate promising accuracy, but they require a lot of labeled data for effective training. Additionally, the analysis uncovers significant gaps in dataset standardization and model interpretability, which pose challenges to the broader applicability of these predictive models.

### Conclusion

In this review, we emphasize the potential of machine learning in HPI prediction and highlight the important challenges that must be addressed to improve predictive accuracy. Unlike previous reviews, our study systematically compares different computational approaches, offering a roadmap for future research. The findings emphasize the importance of dataset quality, feature selection, and model transparency in advancing AI-driven pathogen research.

Keywords
--------

Host-pathogen interactions

Protein-protein interaction

Prediction algorithms

Machine learning algorithms

Bioinformatics

1. Introduction
---------------

The factors contributing to the disease are highly diverse regarding biochemical structure and genetic makeup. Due to minor changes in dynamic ecological balances, these factors stem from various sources and only occasionally surpass their threshold. Climatic changes and deviations from ecological equilibrium, along with other yet undiscovered dimensions, lead to the emergence of new infectious diseases ([Lahlali et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0065)). These instances render widespread outbreaks more probable. In order to combat and prevent future pandemics, researchers are striving to enhance their understanding of infectious diseases, particularly the interaction between pathogens (pathogenic microorganisms) and hosts ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)). Host-pathogen interaction (HPI) is a term used to describe how pathogens, such as viruses, bacteria, prion, fungus, and viroid, interact with their hosts. Pathogens are able to adapt to changes and find different strategies to survive and infect the host ([Poluri et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0100)). These infectious agents are responsible for causing diseases when the host's immune system is unable to mount an effective defense. Investigating host-pathogen interactions provides insights into critical questions regarding pathogen functionality, mechanisms of entry into the host through biological barriers, and their ability to persist in hosts that may be undergoing treatment or vaccination against them ([Syed et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0140)). The dynamics of host and pathogen interactions can be analyzed at various levels: population level (such as viral infections within human populations), organismal level (where pathogens invade individual hosts), and molecular level (including the binding of pathogen proteins to receptors on human cells) ([Sen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0115)). This field began with scattered research on a pathogen and its interaction with the host. The initial investigations into host-pathogen interactions considered environmental factors such as light, temperature, season, and the population of pathogens- hosts ([Syed et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0140)). Later on, organisms such as _C. elegans_ and _D. melanogaster_ were identified as model organisms for studying pathogen behavior in other complex hosts (such as humans) due to their simple body plan, well-known genome structure, and short life cycle. Specific proteins and protein clusters were gradually identified to participate in host-pathogen interactions ([Tecle and Troemel, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0155); [Henry et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0045)). Furthermore, with recent advancements in imaging techniques and molecular biology, specific classifications for the mechanisms of host-pathogen interactions have emerged ([Gómez Borrego and Torrent Burgas, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0040)). Researchers in this field are attempting to use new techniques to examine and identify interactions between a pathogen and its host. Pathogens infiltrate host cells to replicate and spread, engaging in a series of protein-protein interactions (PPIs) with the host. These interactions are typically termed host-pathogen interactions ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)).

As an example of host-pathogen interactions, the mechanism of SARS-CoV-2 entry into human cells can be highlighted. The virus utilizes its spike (S) protein to bind to the ACE2 receptor on human cells, including those in the lungs, heart, and intestines. This binding occurs via the receptor-binding domain (RBD) within the S1 subunit, facilitating viral entry. Subsequently, host proteases, such as TMPRSS2, cleave the spike protein at specific sites, activating the S2 subunit, which promotes membrane fusion and enables the release of the viral RNA genome into the cytoplasm of the host cell ([Steiner et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0135)). Mutations such as N501Y enhance RBD binding affinity, increasing viral transmissibility ([Sarkar and Madabhavi, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0105)). Furthermore, viral binding to ACE2 reduces receptor levels, disrupting angiotensin II degradation and exacerbating inflammatory responses such as cytokine storms ([Bar-On et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0010)). These molecular mechanisms underscore the complexity of host-pathogen interactions and highlight the significance of investigating these interactions for developing effective therapeutic strategies.

Understanding the biological mechanisms of entry and proliferation of pathogens may contribute to developing broad strategies for combating diseases. However, it should be noted that identifying host-pathogen interactions at the mechanistic level is often a laborious, experimental, and costly endeavor. Moreover, due to the adaptability of pathogens, especially viruses, deciphering host-pathogen interaction mechanisms is akin to pursuing a moving target ([Lahlali et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0065)). One of the tools that has given researchers hope in the timely identification of host-pathogen interactions is machine learning algorithms and artificial intelligence. While empirical validation and evaluation remain imperative, computational techniques in the field of artificial intelligence may significantly reduce the number of experiments required to identify host-pathogen interactions through prediction ([Yang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0190)). The field of host-pathogen interactions predicts an important role in designing rational therapeutic interventions, such as drugs. It's possible for experimental methods to be time-consuming, cumbersome, and costly. Testing every possibility can take a significant amount of time. Machine learning can assist prediction methods in overcoming such challenges. They can initially be used to predict hypothetical data that fulfills specific conditions. Then, the predicted set can be empirically validated, requiring much less time and resources ([Kshirsagar et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0060)).

2. Research background
----------------------

### 2.1. Predicting host-pathogen interactions

The interactions between hosts and pathogens are determinant factors in the dynamics of infectious diseases. These interactions are mainly driven by protein-protein interactions (PPIs), which govern each phase of disease progression, starting from the initial invasion of the pathogen to its eventual establishment within the host ([Ul Qamar et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0165)). Proteins are large macromolecules made up of chains of amino acids. Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are essential for nearly all cellular biological and biochemical processes. The majority of proteins carry out their functions through interactions with other proteins. These functions encompass nutrient transport, enzymatic reactions, immune responses, cell-to-cell communication, and tissue regeneration. Therefore, precise PPI identification is essential for understanding cellular physiology ([Soleymani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0130)). In other words, predicting host-pathogen interactions involves predicting protein-protein interactions between the host and the pathogen based on recognizing protein sequences and infectious phenotypes (signs and symptoms) ([Liu-Wei et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0075)).

Moreover, monitoring and analyzing protein-protein interactions (PPIs) can yield valuable candidates for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in medicine, thereby aiding in the development of new drugs ([Trepte et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0160)). Therefore, the challenge of predicting PPIs is a fundamental research topic in systems biology and has thus garnered increased attention in recent years.

Various computational models have been proposed to overcome these drawbacks so that protein-protein interaction pairs can be systematically identified ([Hu et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0050)). In other words, the most extensive method by which a pathogen interacts with a host is Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI). In order to attack the host, pathogen proteins interact with host proteins. The host and its environment can be affected by pathogen proteins in different ways. They can directly interact with host protein(s), affecting subsequent reaction cascades and impairing the host's natural function(s). They can even jeopardize the host's immune defense by misleading and weakening it. They may exploit elements of a collapsing anaerobic environment in the damaged host from immune system attacks. Therefore, predicting potential PPIs between pathogens and their host(s) is of utmost importance ([Durmuş Tekir et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0035)). Various computational exist to predict whether a host protein can interact with a pathogenic protein or vice versa. Current methods for predicting PPIs are generally classified into two categories: laboratory-based and computational ([Murmu et al., 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0090)). Laboratory based methods have their own set of limitations. For instance, in experiments the first issue related to PPIs is usually the time-consuming and effort-intensive nature of these controls, which do not always lead to effective detection of the protein-protein interactions (PPI). Second, the PPI data obtained from these methods are in general incomplete imposed by the fact that assays have their limitations. Lastly, sufficient evidence for high rates of false positives and the like are among predictive results ([Yin et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0195)).

One of these approaches is the prediction based on physicochemical information. This information includes molecular weight, electric charge, solubility, isoelectric point, etc. The physicochemical information-based approach for predicting host-pathogen protein interactions is widely used due to its simplicity. However, this method has weaknesses because the predicted interactions based on this approach may not be significantly reliable due to neglecting other biological features. Therefore, there is a need for more information to increase prediction accuracy ([Tahir et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0145)). Another predictive method is structure-based prediction. When two proteins exhibit structural similarities to a known interacting protein pair, it can be inferred that these proteins may interact in a comparable manner. Another approach is motif-based prediction, which predicts the probability of interaction between two proteins based on the presence of specific motifs in their structure or sequence ([Palhamkhani et al., 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0095)). Motifs are small parts of protein structures or sequences that have specific functions; for example, specific motifs may be responsible for binding to other proteins. By identifying and recognizing similar motifs in proteins, it is possible to predict whether these proteins may interact with each other or not ([Sen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0115)).

### 2.2. Machine learning algorithms

The field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) emerged from efforts to create frameworks for machines with “intelligent features.” Machine Learning (ML), a subset of AI, has greatly contributed to this development by providing practical tools. Typically, a machine learning model is trained on a dataset containing features and variables that influence the target problem, using either supervised or unsupervised methods. Supervised learning involves independent variables (X) and dependent variables (Y), where Y represents a known target feature. These supervised learning techniques are particularly effective for automating the analysis of biological data, where defining precise rules for relevant events can be challenging. ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)).

In recent years, the development of machine learning algorithms has led to the emergence of new methodologies. Alongside classical machine learning algorithms, deep learning algorithms can be considered as a sophisticated evolution of machine learning algorithms. Deep learning has become highly prominent today, and the reason for this can be attributed to achieving results that seemed impossible before ([Chen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0025)).

In recent years, machine learning and deep learning have gained significant attention as subsets of artificial intelligence. Machine learning enables algorithms to be autonomously adapted and enhanced based on incoming data, generating desired outputs without the need for human intervention. Deep learning, which is a subset of machine learning, employs multiple algorithms that each provide a different interpretation of the input data. This hierarchical structure is referred to as artificial neural networks, which endeavor to emulate the functionality of neural networks found in the human brain ([Mahapatra and Sahu, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0080)). Machine learning techniques were only occasionally utilized in the analysis of interactions between pathogens and their hosts in the field of biology before artificial intelligence was utilized. The use of machine learning and artificial intelligence techniques for analyzing host-pathogen interactions has become increasingly important due to the emergence of new diseases. One specific application of machine learning algorithms is the investigation of pathogens and protein interaction sequences to predict host-pathogen interactions, which has garnered significant interest among researchers ([Kaundal et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0055)).

The field of PPI prediction has been well-developed using machine learning models due to the availability of a vast amount of protein data generated by advanced technologies in recent years. Some researchers, such as Tang et al. provide a comprehensive review of machine learning-based prediction methods. The machine learning models used in these methods and the details of protein data representation in their research are specified. The discussion of the development process of machine learning-based methods has been raised to understand the potential advancements in predicting PPIs ([Tang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0150)).

3. Research procedure
---------------------

In this research, considering the importance of utilizing new approaches in predicting host-pathogen interactions, efforts have been made to examine some recent studies in the field of host-pathogen interaction prediction using machine learning algorithms. To this end, the reviewed research has been analyzed and investigated in terms of the data sets used, the type of host and pathogen, the type of algorithm employed for prediction, independent variables for prediction, model evaluation indices, and how research results are presented. This research aims to categorize recent studies in the field of host-pathogen interaction to serve as a suitable guide for researchers. For this purpose, A systematic and step-by-step process was employed to collect articles related to the topic of “Prediction of Host”. The study identification and selection process has been illustrated in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#f0005), following the PRISMA framework.

Fig. 1. Flowchart of Study Identification and Selection (PRISMA Guidelines).

Pathogen Interactions Using Machine Learning Algorithms.“ Initially, a preliminary search for articles related to the topic was conducted using reputable scientific databases such as PubMed, IEEE Xplore, and Google Scholar. In this stage, key phrases such as “Host-Pathogen Interactions, Host-Microbe Interaction Prediction, Host-viruses interaction prediction, protein-protein interaction prediction, Machine Learning Algorithms, Computational Biology, Pathogen Identification, Bioinformatics, Disease Prediction” were searched within the timeframe of 2019 to 2024. To ensure the relevance and quality of the selected studies, specific filters were applied during the literature search. The inclusion criteria were:
*   (1)studies published between 2019 and 2024, 
*   (2)research articles and review papers related to host-pathogen interaction prediction using machine learning, 
*   (3)studies that provided performance metrics for model evaluation, and. 
*   (4)articles available in reputable databases such as PubMed, IEEE Xplore, and Google Scholar. 

The exclusion criteria included:
*   (1)non-English articles, 
*   (2)studies without sufficient methodological details, and. 
*   (3)preprints or unpublished works without peer review.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Statistical methods such as differential expression analysis are vital for identifying significant gene and pathway involvement in host-pathogen interactions, providing insights into disease mechanisms.
Machine learning algorithms enhance the prediction of host-pathogen interactions by analyzing protein-protein interactions, thus streamlining the identification of potential therapeutic targets.

Comparative studies leverage next-generation sequencing to assess variations in host responses to different pathogens, facilitating a deeper understanding of infection dynamics and treatment efficacy.
